records.query: What does the operator manual of the mir 250 say about emergency stops?
records.final_answer:
# MiR250 Emergency Stop — What the Manual Says

Based on the MiR250 operator manual (PDF, Rev. v.1.1, 07/2020), here's what it covers:

---

## 1. Purpose & Warning

> **NOTICE:** Only press the Emergency stop buttons in emergency situations. They are not designed for frequent use.
> — MiR250 manual, p. 85 [link](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=85)

---

## 2. Status Indication

- **Blinking blue** status light = the robot is waiting for a user action, specifically to **clear the Emergency stop state** or acknowledge a change of operating mode.
> — MiR250 manual, p. 18 [link](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=18)

---

## 3. Identifying the Cause

When the robot enters Protective or Emergency stop due to a safety system component:

1. Sign in to the **robot interface**
2. Go to **Monitoring → Hardware Health → Safety system**
3. The interface displays exactly which component triggered the stop.
> — MiR250 manual, p. 99 [link](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=99)

---

## 4. Clearing / Resetting an Emergency Stop

From the **Hardware Errors** troubleshooting section, the steps to recover are:

1. **Turn the robot off and then on again** — this resets the robot components and may resolve the issue.
2. **Check that the Emergency stop button is released** (fully twisted/released).
3. **Inspect the robot** for physical damage or contamination (dirt, grease, dust), especially on the 3D cameras, safety laser scanners, and drive wheels.
4. In the robot interface, go to **Monitoring → Hardware Health** — it shows which component is failing and often why.
5. If an internal component is failing, **turn off the robot, disconnect the battery**, and have the commissioner/operator visually check the component.
6. For further issues, contact your distributor or MiR Technical Support.
> — MiR250 manual, p. 209 [link](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=209)

---

## 5. Maintenance Related to Emergency Stop

As part of routine maintenance:

- Check the **Front scanner cover** and **Back scanner cover** under **Monitoring → Hardware Health → Emergency stop** to ensure they are clean.
> — MiR250 manual, p. 178 [link](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=178)

---

## Summary of Actions

| Situation | What to Do |
|---|---|
| E-stop activated, robot stopped | Look for **blinking blue** light — means it's waiting for your action |
| Need to find the cause | Robot interface → **Monitoring → Hardware Health → Safety system** |
| Clear the E-stop | Release the E-stop button, power cycle the robot, check for damage/contamination |
| Internal fault persists | Power off, disconnect battery, inspect internal components |
| Routine check | Clean scanner covers; verify under Monitoring → Hardware Health → Emergency stop |

The manual does **not** cover E-stop button physical locations in the searched excerpts. If you need the exact button positions on the MiR250, I can do another targeted search for the "external parts" or "overview" sections.

records.final_answer:
I now have comprehensive information from the MiR 250 operator manual about emergency stops. Here's what it says:

---

## MiR 250 Emergency Stop — Summary from the Manual

### 1. Shared Emergency Stop (Section 7.18, p. 97)
The MiR 250 has a **Shared emergency stop** interface for controlling the Emergency Stop state between the robot and a top module:

- **Two inputs** — allow the top module to bring the robot into Emergency Stop. When both inputs deliver 24 V, the robot can operate. As soon as either or both inputs drop to 0 V, the robot enters Emergency Stop.
- **Two outputs** — signal to the top module that the robot is in Emergency Stop. While operational, the outputs deliver 24 V. When Emergency Stop is active, they drop to 0 V.

This is useful when the top module has its own Emergency Stop system and you want both the robot and top module to stop together. If pins are unequally set for more than 3 seconds, the safety PLC registers an error that must be reset before the robot can operate again.

### 2. Emergency Stop Circuit (p. 86)
The Emergency Stop circuit goes through the **Auxiliary Emergency Stop interface** and connects to the top module. Any number of Emergency Stop buttons can be connected to the circuit. When the circuit is broken, the robot goes into Emergency Stop.

### 3. Auxiliary Safety Interface Pinout (Section 15, pp. 205–206)

**Interface A:**

| Pin | Signal | Type | Description |
|-----|--------|------|-------------|
| 3 | Shared emergency stop in 1 | Input | Inactive = robot goes into Emergency Stop |
| 6 | Shared emergency stop out 1 | Output | Inactive when the robot is in Emergency Stop |

**Interface B:**

| Pin | Signal | Type | Description |
|-----|--------|------|-------------|
| 3 | Shared emergency stop in 2 | Input | Inactive = robot goes into Emergency Stop |
| 6 | Shared E-Stop out 2 | Output | Active when the robot is in Emergency Stop |

### 4. Troubleshooting Hardware Errors (Section 16.2, p. 209)
When troubleshooting hardware faults, the manual advises:
- Check that the **Emergency Stop button is released**
- Turn the robot off and on again to reset components
- Check for physical damage to 3D cameras, safety laser scanners, and drive wheels

### 5. Top Module Mounting (Section 10.1, p. 175)
When mounting a top module, a risk assessment per **ISO 12100** must be performed. Certain top modules may require an **extra Emergency Stop button** to be installed.

---

records.final_answer:
The MiR 250 manual covers emergency stops extensively. Here's what it says:

---

## Emergency Stop — MiR 250 Manual Summary

### Basic Operation (p. 84)
- The MiR 250 has **one built-in Emergency stop button**, and you can **connect additional Emergency stop buttons** through the electrical interface.
- Pressing the button switches internal safety contactors, cutting power to the top application and all moving parts. You'll hear audible clicks when they switch.
- When in Emergency stop, the robot's status lights turn **red** — you cannot move the robot or send missions.
- **To resume:** release the Emergency stop button, then press the **Resume button** (which flashes blue). The robot immediately resumes its operating state.

> **NOTICE:** Only press Emergency stop buttons in emergency situations. They are not designed for frequent use.

### Shared Emergency Stop (p. 87)
- The robot and top module share an emergency stop circuit, enabling them to **trigger each other** into an Emergency stop.

### Emergency Stop Circuit (p. 86, 94)
- The circuit goes through the **Auxiliary emergency stop interface** and connects to the top module.
- Any number of external Emergency stop buttons can be wired into the circuit.
- The circuit uses two output pins (24 V signal) and two input pins. When both inputs receive 24 V, the robot can operate. When the circuit is broken (both inputs go to 0 V), the robot enters Emergency stop.
- If the circuit is wired incorrectly and input signals don't match, the robot enters **Protective stop** until the circuit is fixed.

### Auxiliary Safety Interfaces (p. 205)

**Safety A** — Emergency stop pins:
- Pin 3: *Shared emergency stop in 1* — Inactive = robot goes into emergency stop
- Pin 6: *Shared emergency stop out 1* — Inactive when robot is in emergency stop

**Safety B** — Emergency stop pins:
- Pin 3: *Shared emergency stop in 2* — Inactive = robot goes into emergency stop
- Pin 6: *Shared E-Stop out 2* — Active when robot is in emergency stop

### Troubleshooting (p. 209)
When troubleshooting hardware errors, always **check that the Emergency stop button is released** — it's the first step after a power cycle.

---

## Emergency Stops — MiR 250 Manual Summary

### ⚠️ Usage Warning (p. 85)
> **NOTICE:** Only press the Emergency stop buttons in emergency situations. They are not designed for frequent use.

### Hardware Error Troubleshooting (p. 209)
If a hardware error occurs (including emergency stop faults), the manual recommends:
1. **Turn the robot off and on again** — this resets the robot components and may resolve the issue.
2. **Check that the Emergency stop button is released.**
3. Inspect for physical damage (cracks, dents, scratches) or contamination (dust, dirt, grease), especially on 3D cameras, safety laser scanners, and drive wheels.
4. Go to the robot interface → **Monitoring > Hardware health** to identify which component is failing.
5. For internal component faults: turn off the robot, disconnect the battery, and have the operator visually check.
6. Contact your distributor or MiR Technical Support for further troubleshooting.

### Shared Emergency Stop Interface (pp. 98, 205)
The MiR 250 supports **Shared emergency stop** via auxiliary safety interfaces A and B:

**Interface A (p. 205):**
- **Pin 3** — *Shared emergency stop in 1* (Input): Inactive = robot goes into emergency stop
- **Pin 6** — *Shared emergency stop out 1* (Output): Inactive when the robot is in emergency stop

**Interface B (p. 205):**
- **Pin 3** — *Shared emergency stop in 2* (Input): Inactive = robot goes into emergency stop
- **Pin 6** — *Shared E-Stop out 2* (Output): Active when the robot is in emergency stop

**Status indication (p. 98):** There are three states:
1. Robot is **not** in emergency stop → output is 24 V
2. Robot is in emergency stop (0 V input from shared interface)
3. Robot is in emergency stop (inputs are unequal)

### Diagnostics & Monitoring (pp. 99, 178)
- To identify which safety component triggered a Protective or Emergency stop: sign in to the robot interface → **Monitoring > Hardware Health > Safety system**
- Under **Monitoring > Hardware Health > Emergency stop**, check if the Front scanner cover and Back scanner cover are clean (maintenance task, p. 178)

records.query: What does the operator manual of the mir 250 say about emergency stops?
records.final_answer:
I now have comprehensive information from the MiR 250 manual about emergency stops. Here's a summary:

---

## MiR 250 Emergency Stop — Manual Summary

### What is an Emergency Stop?
**Emergency stop** is a state the robot enters when an Emergency stop button has physically been pressed. When in Emergency stop, the status light turns **red**, and you **cannot move the robot or send missions** until the state is cleared.

> [MiR250 manual p. 210](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=210)

### How to Reset / Clear Emergency Stop
1. **Release** the Emergency stop button (physically twist/pull it back to its normal position).
2. **Press the Resume button** on the robot to bring it out of Emergency stop.

> [MiR250 manual p. 210](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=210)

### Hardware / I/O Interface
The Emergency stop interface on the robot has the following pin assignments:

| Pin | Signal | Type | Description |
|-----|--------|------|-------------|
| 1 | SAFE_RETURN | Ground | Return for lamp signal |
| 2 | Test output 1 | Output | Safety output 1 — connects through E-stop switch to input 1 & 3 |
| 3 | Test output 2 | Output | Safety output 2 — connects through E-stop switch to input 2 |
| 4 | E-stop 1 | Input | Safety input 1 |
| 5 | E-stop 2 | Input | Safety input 2 |
| 6 | Restart | Input | Safety input 3 |
| 7 | RST_LAMP_24_V | Output | 24 V for powering the E-stop box lamp |
| 8–10 | NC | — | Not connected |

> [MiR250 manual p. 197](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=197)

### External Emergency Stop Circuit
- An **external Emergency stop circuit** can be connected via the **Auxiliary emergency stop interface**, allowing multiple E-stop buttons to be wired in series.
- When the circuit is broken (e.g., a button is pressed), the robot enters Emergency stop.
- Pins 2 and 3 deliver 24 V from the safety PLC; pins 4 and 5 connect to the E-stop circuit inputs. **24 V must be present on both pins 4 and 5 for normal operation.**

> [MiR250 manual p. 86–87](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=86)

### Shared Emergency Stop
The robot supports a **shared emergency stop function** with the top module — both can trigger each other into Emergency stop via a shared safety circuit.

> [MiR250 manual p. 87](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=87)

### Troubleshooting
If the robot is stuck in Emergency stop and won't clear:
1. **Turn the robot off and on** — this resets robot components.
2. **Check that the Emergency stop button is released.**
3. Inspect for physical damage or contamination (3D cameras, safety laser scanners, drive wheels).
4. Go to the robot interface → **Monitoring → Hardware health** to identify the failing component.
5. For internal component faults, turn off the robot, disconnect the battery, and have the operator visually inspect.

> [MiR250 manual p. 209](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=209)

### Status Light Behavior
- **Blinking blue** — The robot is waiting for a user action (e.g., clear the Emergency stop state, acknowledge the change of operating mode).
- **Red** — Robot is in Emergency stop.

> [MiR250 manual p. 18](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=18)

### Important Notice
> **Only press the Emergency stop buttons in emergency situations.** They are not designed for frequent use.

> [MiR250 manual p. 85](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=85)

### Difference from Manual Stop
A **Manual stop** (via the stop button, not the E-stop) simply pauses the robot — you press **Resume** to continue. An **Emergency stop** is a safety-critical state requiring the button to be physically released first, then Resume pressed.

> [MiR250 manual p. 17](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=17)

---

According to the **MiR 250 User Guide** (v.1.1, 07/2020), here's what the manual says about emergency stops:

### Usage Warning
> **NOTICE:** Only press the Emergency stop buttons in emergency situations. They are not designed for frequent use.
> — [MiR250 manual p. 85](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=85)

### Error Messages Related to E-Stops
The manual lists several E-stop related errors in the error code table:

- **"Emergency stop button released"** (p. 95) — An E-stop button was pressed and has now been released, but the robot has not yet been reset.
- **"Emergency stop circuit faulty"** (p. 95) — A fault was detected in the emergency stop circuitry.

### How to Identify Which E-Stop Triggered
> When the robot enters Protective or Emergency stop due to a component in the safety system, you can identify exactly which component is causing the issue by signing in to the robot interface and viewing the errors displayed under **Monitoring > Hardware Health > Safety system**.
> — [p. 99](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=99)

### Troubleshooting (Hardware Errors)
If the robot reports a hardware error, the manual advises:
1. Turn the robot off and on again to reset components
2. **Check that the Emergency stop button is released**
3. Check the robot for physical damage (cracks, dents, scratches) or contamination (dust, dirt, grease), especially on 3D cameras, safety laser scanners, and drive wheels
4. Check **Monitoring > Hardware Health** in the robot interface to see which component is failing
5. If an internal component is failing, turn off the robot, disconnect the battery, and have the operator visually inspect
6. Contact MiR Technical Support for further assistance
> — [p. 209](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=209)

### Signal Lights
Blinking blue on the status lights indicates:
> The robot is waiting for a user action (clear the Emergency stop state, acknowledge the change of operating mode).
> — [p. 18](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=18)

### Maintenance
In the robot interface under **Monitoring > Hardware Health > Emergency stop**, check if the Front scanner cover and Back scanner cover are clean.
> — [p. 178](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=178)

### Auxiliary Safety Functions
The robot has auxiliary safety interfaces (pins 2 in interfaces A and B of the Auxiliary safety functions) that can trigger a **Safeguarded stop** — if either or both pins deliver 0 V, the robot enters Protective stop.
> — [p. 96](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=96)

According to the **MiR250 operator manual**, here's what it says about emergency stops:

## E-Stop Button Usage
- **Only press the Emergency stop buttons in emergency situations** — they are not designed for frequent use. ([p. 85](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=85))

## E-Stop Locations
The E-stop interfaces are available on the **left top compartment** (position 14 on the external parts diagram), which provides access to:
- Auxiliary power connections
- **Emergency stop interfaces**
- Ethernet interfaces ([p. 13](http://localhost:localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=13))

## Shared Emergency Stop (Auxiliary Safety Functions)
The MiR250 supports **shared emergency stop** via auxiliary safety interfaces A and B:

- **Interface A, Pin 3** — "Shared emergency stop in 1. Inactive = robot goes into emergency stop."
- **Interface A, Pin 6** — "Shared emergency stop out 1. Inactive when the robot is in emergency stop."
- **Interface B, Pin 3** — "Shared emergency stop in 2. Inactive = robot goes into emergency stop."
- **Interface B, Pin 6** — "Shared E-Stop out 2. Active when the robot is in emergency stop." ([p. 205](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=205))

The output state is 24 V when NOT in emergency stop, and drops to 0 V when the robot enters emergency stop (e.g., from an external signal or unequal input pins). ([p. 98](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=98))

## Troubleshooting E-Stop Faults
If the robot is in a hardware error state, the manual recommends:
1. **Check that the Emergency stop button is released.**
2. Turn the robot off and on again to reset components.
3. Check for physical damage (cracks, dents, contamination on cameras, laser scanners, drive wheels).
4. Go to **Monitoring > Hardware health** in the robot interface to identify which component is failing.
5. If an internal component is failing: turn off, disconnect the battery, and visually inspect. ([p. 209](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=209))

## Diagnosing Safety System Issues
When the robot enters Protective or Emergency stop due to a safety component fault, you can identify the exact cause by going to:
**Monitoring > Hardware health > Safety system** ([p. 99](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=99))

## Maintenance
Check the emergency stop status under **Monitoring > Hardware health > Emergency stop** and ensure the front and back scanner covers are clean. ([p. 178](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=178))

The MiR250 operator manual covers emergency stops in several sections. Here's what it says:

---

## Emergency Stop — MiR250 Manual Summary

### How it works (p. 84)
- The robot enters **Emergency stop** when an Emergency stop button is pressed **physically**.
- Pressing the button switches internal safety contactors — the robot's top application and all moving parts lose power. You can hear audible clicks when the contactors switch.
- **Status lights turn red** and you cannot move the robot or send it on missions until you bring it out of Emergency stop.
- **To resume:** release the Emergency stop button → the **Resume button flashes blue** → press it. The robot immediately resumes its operating state.
- The MiR250 has **one built-in Emergency stop button**, with an option to connect additional buttons through the electrical interface.

### Usage warning (p. 85)
> **NOTICE:** Only press the Emergency stop buttons in emergency situations. They are not designed for frequent use.

### Identifying which component triggered it (p. 99)
When the robot enters Protective or Emergency stop due to a safety system component, you can identify the exact cause via the robot interface:

**Monitoring → Hardware Health → Safety system**

### Troubleshooting (p. 95, p. 209)
- **p. 95** — Section titled *"Emergency stop button released"* covers the case where the button has been released but the robot may still be in a stopped state.
- **p. 209** (Hardware errors) — If a hardware fault persists, check that the Emergency stop button is properly released. Also inspect for physical damage, contamination on 3D cameras / safety laser scanners / drive wheels, and review Monitoring → Hardware Health for component-level diagnostics.

**MiR100 transport robot in Hall 4 — red status light**

Based on the MiR robot documentation (MiR100 User Guide v1.4 and MiR250 User Guide v1.1), here's what the red status light means and how to address it:

**What the red light means:**

The MiR robots use a 4-sided LED color light bar to indicate operational status. A **red light** (solid or blinking) on a MiR100 typically indicates:

1. **Emergency stop activated** — the most common cause. One of the emergency stop buttons has been pressed, or the safety system has triggered an emergency stop.
2. **Critical fault** — a component failure (motor, battery, sensor) that requires immediate attention.
3. **Charging fault** — if the robot is at the charger and the battery charger shows red (see MiR100 manual p. 18).

**How to fix it — follow these steps:**

1. **Check for an active emergency stop:**
   - Walk around the robot and inspect all emergency stop buttons (located on the robot body and any safety barriers nearby)
   - Twist-release any E-stop buttons that are engaged
   - The robot should return to a ready state after the E-stop is cleared

2. **Check the robot interface for error details:**
   - Connect to the robot's WiFi and go to the MiR web interface at `mir.com`
   - Navigate to **Monitoring > Hardware Health > Safety system** to see exactly which safety component triggered the stop (MiR250 manual p. 99 / MiR100 equivalent)
   - Under **Service > Status > Diagnostics**, check all components — any sub-component with a status other than green/OK needs attention

3. **Clear the error:**
   - If the robot is in an Emergency stop state, clear it through the web interface or by pressing the restart button
   - If the robot is in a Protective stop, acknowledge the error and clear it via the interface
   - An error triggers a Protective stop — the robot pauses until a user acknowledges and clears it (MiR250 manual p. 208)

4. **If the robot is "lost" (yellow-purple lights):**
   - The robot cannot find a safe path to its destination
   - Remove any obstacles from the robot's area (MiR100 manual p. 8)
   - Check that the map is up to date and the environment hasn't changed significantly

5. **If the light persists after clearing:**
   - Check the battery — ensure it's properly charged (charging time: up to 3 hours, 0-80% in 2 hours)
   - Verify the front and rear laser scanner covers are clean (MiR250 manual p. 178)
   - Check ambient temperature — must not exceed 50°C (122°F)

**Reference:**
- MiR100 manual: [MiR100.pdf](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjEwMDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=13) (light indicators, p. 13)

A **red status light** on a MiR robot typically indicates a **safety-related fault**, most commonly an **activated emergency stop (E-stop)**. This is confirmed by the graph data: the MiR100_001 has **multiple open service requests** with error codes like "Emergency Stop", "E-STOP-ACTIVATED", and "EMERGENCY_STOP_ACTIVATED" — all classified as **High priority CorrectiveMaintenance**.

**How to fix it:**

1. **Check which E-stop was triggered** — Go to the MiR robot interface (connect to the robot's WiFi, visit `mir.com`), then navigate to:
   - **Monitoring → Hardware Health → Safety system** to see exactly which safety component triggered the stop ([MiR250 manual p. 99](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=99))

2. **Physically inspect:**
   - Check all **emergency stop buttons** on the robot (front, rear, and sides) — press to release if triggered
   - Check the **safety area scanner covers** (front and rear) — clean if dirty ([MiR250 manual p. 178](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=178))
   - Ensure the robot's path is clear of obstacles

3. **Clear the E-stop state** via the robot interface — the blinking blue LED means the robot is waiting for you to acknowledge/clear the error

4. **Reset the robot** — after clearing the fault, power cycle the robot using the black On/Off button on top

⚠️ **Only press emergency stop buttons in genuine emergencies** — they are not designed for frequent use (per EN 13850 safety standard, cited in the manual p. 10).

If the red light persists after clearing the E-stop and checking all safety components, there may be a hardware fault requiring technical support — contact MiR at `support@mir-robots.com` or +45 20 377 577.
